findings.summary: The single Settings class in common/config.py owns configuration for both the OCR and classifier domains, creating a hub module that couples every consumer to every setting. OCR-specific settings (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS, OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, REFUSAL_MARK) and classifier-specific settings (CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, etc.) are mixed into one flat class.
evidence: Settings has 30+ attributes spanning both domains. The classifier daemon loads OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS even though it never uses them, and the OCR daemon loads CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, etc. Any change to classifier settings requires the OCR daemon's Settings to still validate.
findings.suggestion: Split into a base CommonSettings plus OcrSettings and ClassifierSettings subclasses (or composition), so each daemon only loads and validates its own configuration.

findings.summary: Refusal/error phrase detection is defined in two independent places with overlapping but divergent phrase lists, creating a subtle boundary violation between ocr and classifier.
evidence: common/config.py defines OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS (configurable via env) and REFUSAL_MARK. classifier/constants.py defines a hardcoded ERROR_PHRASES tuple with overlapping but different phrases (e.g. "i'm sorry, i can't assist with that." only in classifier). Both are fed to the same is_error_content() function in common/utils.py. The OCR worker also uses a one-element tuple (self.settings.REFUSAL_MARK,) separately from OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate refusal/error phrase lists into a single shared source in common/ so both pipelines use the same detection logic and phrase set.

findings.summary: The preflight module bypasses the project's own OpenAI client abstraction and uses the global openai module directly, creating an implicit dependency on module-level openai SDK state.
evidence: preflight.py line 74 calls openai.models.list() using the global openai module, while the rest of the codebase carefully initializes a dedicated openai.OpenAI client in library_setup.py and stores it in llm._openai_client. The preflight check relies on openai SDK global state (which may not be configured the same way as the explicit client, especially for Ollama providers).
findings.suggestion: Use the initialized _openai_client from llm.py (or accept a client parameter) instead of the global openai module, so the preflight check uses the same configuration as the rest of the application.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient.get_documents_to_process() is a domain-aware method on a shared boundary class that is never called by any consumer.
evidence: get_documents_to_process() at paperless.py:103 uses self.settings.PRE_TAG_ID (an OCR-specific tag) and is never called anywhere in the codebase. Both daemons use iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag() from document_iter.py which calls get_documents_by_tag() directly.
findings.suggestion: Remove get_documents_to_process() from PaperlessClient. It embeds OCR-specific domain logic in a shared infrastructure class and is dead code.

findings.summary: The OCR daemon defines _process_document as a module-level function in daemon.py, while the classifier daemon defines process_document as a nested closure inside main(). This is an inconsistent pattern for the same architectural role.
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 18: 'def _process_document(doc: dict, settings: Settings) -> None:' is a module-level private function. classifier/daemon.py line 66: 'def process_document(doc: dict) -> None:' is a closure inside main() that captures settings, taxonomy_cache, etc. via closure scope.
findings.suggestion: Pick one pattern and apply it to both daemons. The closure approach in the classifier is justified by its extra dependency (taxonomy_cache), but a consistent pattern (e.g. both as closures, or both as module-level functions taking explicit dependencies) would be clearer.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider resets stats at the start of each classify_text() call, while OcrProvider accumulates stats across all transcribe_image() calls for the lifetime of the provider instance.
evidence: classifier/provider.py line 131: 'self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS)' is called at the top of classify_text(). OcrProvider.transcribe_image() has no such reset; stats accumulate across all pages/calls. Both providers share ThreadSafeStats from common/llm.py.
findings.suggestion: Decide whether stats represent per-call or per-instance metrics and apply the same strategy to both providers.

findings.summary: The common/__init__.py is effectively empty (single docstring, no exports), while ocr/__init__.py and classifier/__init__.py define explicit __all__ exports. This is inconsistent but not a boundary problem since common is imported by submodule path throughout.
evidence: common/__init__.py contains only a docstring. No consumer imports from 'common' directly (all use 'common.config', 'common.tags', etc.), so the __init__.py re-exports would have value for discoverability. ocr/ and classifier/ both define __all__ with key public names.
findings.suggestion: Either add key re-exports to common/__init__.py for consistency with sibling packages, or document that common is intentionally import-by-submodule only.

findings.summary: common/tags.py's clean_pipeline_tags() knows about every pipeline's tag IDs (PRE_TAG_ID, POST_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID, OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, etc.), coupling the shared module to both domain-specific tag schemes.
evidence: clean_pipeline_tags() at tags.py:151-167 explicitly references settings.PRE_TAG_ID, settings.POST_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID, settings.OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_POST_TAG_ID, and settings.ERROR_TAG_ID. Adding a new pipeline would require modifying this shared function.
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs to clean as a parameter (e.g. a set of tag IDs) rather than reaching into Settings for every pipeline's IDs. This would let each daemon specify which tags to clean without coupling the shared module to all pipelines.

findings.summary: Single Settings class owns configuration for both OCR and classifier daemons, coupling unrelated subsystems at the config layer
evidence: Settings.__init__ calls _load_api_settings, _load_llm_settings, _load_tag_settings, _load_daemon_settings, _load_image_settings, _load_logging_settings, and _load_classification_settings unconditionally. The OCR daemon loads CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_MAX_TOKENS, etc. that it never uses. The classifier daemon loads OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, etc. that it never uses. The class has 40+ attributes spanning both domains.
findings.suggestion: Split into a base CommonSettings plus OcrSettings and ClassifierSettings subclasses or composition objects, so each daemon only loads and validates its own configuration. This also makes it clearer which env vars are relevant to which daemon.

findings.summary: The common/ package contains 14 modules spanning HTTP client, retry logic, concurrency, tag lifecycle, claim protocol, shutdown signaling, bootstrap, preflight, logging, library setup, and document iteration — breadth that blurs ownership boundaries
evidence: The common/ package has 15 modules. tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, and document_iter.py form a cohesive tag-lifecycle cluster. paperless.py is the API client. retry.py, concurrency.py are infrastructure. bootstrap.py, preflight.py, shutdown.py, library_setup.py, logging_config.py are startup concerns. A new engineer cannot predict what lives in common/ without reading the directory.
findings.suggestion: Consider sub-packaging: e.g. common/pipeline/ for tag lifecycle (tags, claims, stale_lock, document_iter), common/infra/ for retry, concurrency, shutdown, or at minimum group the tag-lifecycle modules under a sub-package since they form a tight cluster with shared concepts.

findings.summary: Tags are repeatedly converted between set[int] and list[int] at every boundary crossing, creating noisy translation code throughout the pipeline
evidence: extract_tags returns set[int]. update_document_metadata and update_document accept list[int] for tags. Every call site does list(tags) or list(updated): tags.py:67 list(updated), tags.py:107 list(tags), claims.py:53 list(updated_tags), worker.py:200 list(current_tags), classifier/worker.py:196 list(updated), classifier/worker.py:299 list(current_tags). The set-to-list conversion appears at least 10 times across the codebase.
findings.suggestion: Have the PaperlessClient accept Iterable[int] or set[int] for tags and do the list conversion internally, or standardize on one representation throughout the pipeline layer.

findings.summary: Processing tag IDs use None to mean 'not configured' but falsy checks conflate None and 0, and the boundary between 'disabled' and 'absent' is handled inconsistently
evidence: claim_processing_tag line 26: 'if not tag_id: return True' — returns success when tag_id is 0 (an unlikely but valid Paperless ID). release_processing_tag line 90: 'if not tag_id: return' — same pattern. document_iter.py line 63: 'if processing_tag_id and processing_tag_id in tags' — skips when 0. config.py uses _get_optional_positive_int_env which converts <=0 to None, but this implicit contract is not enforced at the type level.
findings.suggestion: Use explicit 'is None' checks instead of truthiness checks for optional tag IDs, since tag IDs are integers where 0 could theoretically be valid. The type annotation already says 'int | None', so the check should match.

findings.summary: The retry decorator requires the decorated method's class to have a 'settings' attribute conforming to RetrySettings protocol, creating an implicit coupling contract
evidence: retry.py line 32-33: 'def wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs): settings: RetrySettings = self.settings' — the decorator silently assumes the first positional arg is self and that self.settings exists. This couples every retried method to a specific object shape. OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion and PaperlessClient._get/_patch/_post both depend on this pattern. If someone decorates a standalone function or a class without .settings, they get an AttributeError with no helpful message.
findings.suggestion: Accept retry parameters explicitly (e.g. as decorator arguments or via a passed-in config) rather than reaching into self.settings. Alternatively, add a clear error message when the protocol is not satisfied.

findings.summary: The OpenAI client singleton pattern requires init() to be called before get(), creating a temporal coupling that is only enforced at runtime
evidence: llm.py _OpenAIClientHolder.get() raises RuntimeError if _client is None. The init is done in library_setup.py's setup_libraries(), which must be called by bootstrap_daemon() before any LLM work happens. If a test or alternative entry point forgets this ordering, they get a cryptic RuntimeError. The holder pattern replaces bare module-level mutable state but retains the temporal coupling problem.
findings.suggestion: Consider lazy initialization (init on first get() if not already done) or make the client a constructor parameter of the classes that need it, rather than relying on module-level singleton state with ordering requirements.

findings.summary: OcrProvider.transcribe_image mutates the caller's PIL Image in place via thumbnail(), creating a hidden side effect
evidence: provider.py line 69: 'image.thumbnail((self.settings.OCR_MAX_SIDE, self.settings.OCR_MAX_SIDE))' — PIL.Image.thumbnail modifies the image in place. The caller (DocumentProcessor._ocr_pages_in_parallel) passes the original images list, which is later iterated for .close(). While the current code works because each image is only transcribed once, the mutation is invisible from the call site and would cause subtle bugs if images were reused.
findings.suggestion: Either document the mutation explicitly in the docstring, or create a resized copy (image.copy().thumbnail(...) or image.resize(...)) to avoid mutating the input parameter.

findings.summary: The daemon polling loop catches only (OSError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError), letting other common exceptions crash the daemon
evidence: daemon_loop.py line 105: 'except (OSError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) as exc:' — This misses httpx.HTTPError (a common failure mode when Paperless is unreachable during fetch_work), KeyError (from malformed API responses), and json.JSONDecodeError. An httpx.TimeoutException during _poll_once's fetch_work call would propagate uncaught and crash the entire daemon loop.
findings.suggestion: Catch Exception at the top-level loop (with explicit re-raise of SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt) to ensure the daemon stays alive through unexpected transient errors. The individual work items already have their own error handling.

findings.summary: DocumentProcessor.process() computes elapsed time after the finally block, so it is unreachable on error paths
evidence: worker.py lines 58-131: start_time is set at line 58, the try block starts at line 60, the finally block runs at lines 116-124, and the elapsed time computation is at lines 126-131. If any exception occurs during processing, the finally block executes but then the exception propagates, so lines 126-131 never run. The elapsed time is only logged for successful completions, which reduces observability for slow failures.
findings.suggestion: Move the elapsed time computation into the finally block so it is always logged, regardless of success or failure.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider.classify_text resets stats at the start of each call, but OcrProvider.transcribe_image never resets — stats accumulate across documents in OCR but not in classification
evidence: classifier/provider.py line 131: 'self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS)' at the start of classify_text. ocr/provider.py: transcribe_image has no reset call — stats accumulate across all pages and all documents processed by the same OcrProvider instance. Since each document gets a new OcrProvider (ocr/daemon.py line 26), accumulation is per-document, but the asymmetry is confusing and would become a bug if provider instances were reused.
findings.suggestion: Either reset stats consistently in both providers at the start of each top-level call, or document the intended accumulation strategy. The inconsistency suggests drift rather than design.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient._create_named_item can fall through without returning when last_response is a non-400 success that also fails raise_for_status in an unexpected way
evidence: paperless.py lines 76-113: The for loop iterates candidates, calls self._post, then tries raise_for_status(). If raise_for_status raises for a non-400 status code, the exception propagates (line 113). But mypy/type checkers would flag that the function has no explicit return after the loop — if candidates is empty (which cannot happen given the logic but is not structurally prevented), the function returns None implicitly despite the return type of dict[str, Any].
findings.suggestion: Add a defensive raise or unreachable assertion after the for loop to satisfy both the type checker and future readers.

findings.summary: OCR error/refusal detection uses two different mechanisms (is_error_content and direct marker checks) with overlapping but not identical coverage
evidence: provider.py line 102 uses is_error_content(text, self.settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS). worker.py line 169-171 _has_ocr_errors checks OCR_ERROR_MARKER in text OR is_error_content(text, (self.settings.REFUSAL_MARK,)) — note this passes only REFUSAL_MARK as a tuple, not the full OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS list. So the worker checks a subset of markers compared to the provider. classifier/quality_gates.py has its own needs_error_tag using ERROR_PHRASES from constants.py. Three different error detection surfaces with overlapping but inconsistent marker sets.
findings.suggestion: Unify error content detection into a single function that accepts the full marker set, or clearly document why different stages check different subsets of markers.

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache holds its RLock during Paperless API calls (create_correspondent, create_tag, etc.), blocking all other threads from taxonomy lookups during potentially slow network operations
evidence: taxonomy.py _get_or_create_item_id lines 187-209: The entire method body runs under 'with self._lock:', including the call to creator() (line 193) which makes an HTTP POST to Paperless. If that call takes 5 seconds due to network latency, all other classification worker threads are blocked from even reading cached taxonomy data. Similarly, refresh() at line 134 holds the lock while making three sequential API calls (list_correspondents, list_document_types, list_tags).
findings.suggestion: Use a read-write lock pattern or reduce the lock scope: acquire the lock to check the cache, release it during the API call, then re-acquire to update the cache. The current code accepts a retry-after-refresh fallback but the lock contention during normal creation is unnecessary.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._supports_temperature uses a hardcoded model name prefix check that will silently break as model names evolve
evidence: provider.py line 39: 'return not model.startswith("gpt-5")' — This assumes gpt-5 models do not support temperature, but future gpt-5.x variants might, and non-gpt-5 models that also don't support temperature (like o1, o3) would incorrectly get temperature set. The compat retry mechanism (_try_strip_compat_param) exists as a safety net, but the hardcoded check creates false confidence.
findings.suggestion: Remove the prefix check and rely entirely on the compat retry mechanism, or make the unsupported-temperature model patterns configurable rather than hardcoded.

findings.summary: The exception types caught at I/O boundaries are inconsistent across modules. tags.py and claims.py catch (OSError, httpx.HTTPError), while preflight.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) for Paperless but (OpenAIAPIError, OSError) for LLM checks. paperless.py catches (httpx.RequestError, httpx.HTTPStatusError) for retries. The stale_lock.py also catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError). Meanwhile, ocr/worker.py catches bare RuntimeError from image conversion and bare Exception from thread pool futures. There is no single exception taxonomy or constant defining the retryable/catchable boundary exceptions, leading to a risk that new call sites will pick a different subset.
evidence: tags.py lines 41,68,94,108 all catch (OSError, httpx.HTTPError). claims.py lines 31,54,65 catch the same tuple. paperless.py line 17 defines RETRYABLE_HTTP_EXCEPTIONS = (httpx.RequestError, httpx.HTTPStatusError) which is a different set (httpx.HTTPStatusError is a subclass of neither OSError nor httpx.HTTPError in the same way). The callers of PaperlessClient methods that already retry internally then also wrap calls in their own try/except with a different exception tuple, creating two layers of inconsistent catch logic.
findings.suggestion: Define a single PAPERLESS_CATCHABLE_EXCEPTIONS constant (or a base exception type) in common/ and use it consistently across all modules that call PaperlessClient methods. This makes the error boundary explicit and prevents drift.

findings.summary: Several functions in tags.py and stale_lock.py catch exceptions on I/O operations, log them, but neither propagate nor return an error indicator to the caller. This means the caller has no way to know the operation failed. For example, release_processing_tag silently returns on failure, and remove_stale_queue_tag logs but does not tell the caller the tag was not removed.
evidence: tags.py release_processing_tag (lines 82-114): catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) at two points and returns silently. remove_stale_queue_tag (lines 53-79): catches and logs but does not propagate. The docstring for release_processing_tag says 'Best-effort, never propagates errors' which is intentional but inconsistent with claim_processing_tag which returns a bool success indicator. stale_lock.py recover_stale_locks (line 54): catches per-document but continues, which is fine, but the outer catch at line 27 returns 0 silently on list failure.
findings.suggestion: For best-effort operations, at minimum return a success bool so callers can optionally react. The asymmetry between claim_processing_tag (returns bool) and release_processing_tag (returns None, swallows errors) is a consistency gap that could mask persistent failures.

findings.summary: The preflight LLM check in _check_llm_reachable() calls openai.models.list() using the module-level openai default client, not the configured _openai_client that was just set up by setup_libraries(). If the module-level client has different configuration (or no configuration), this check may pass or fail for the wrong reasons.
evidence: preflight.py line 74: openai.models.list() uses the module-level default openai client. library_setup.py creates a custom openai.OpenAI client and stores it via init_openai_client(), but that client is only used by OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion (llm.py line 43). The preflight check bypasses this entirely.
findings.suggestion: Either expose the configured client from llm.py and use it in the preflight check, or accept the inconsistency and document it. Currently it is a latent bug for Ollama setups where the module-level client has no valid base_url.

findings.summary: The OCR test file test_worker.py defines local wrapper functions _make_settings, _make_mock_paperless, _make_mock_ocr_provider that simply call the centralized factory/mock functions. The classifier test_worker.py imports the factories directly. This is an inconsistent pattern between sibling test modules.
evidence: test_worker.py (ocr) lines 37-49: defines _make_settings, _make_mock_paperless, _make_mock_ocr_provider as trivial wrappers around tests.helpers.factories/mocks. test_worker.py (classifier) lines 18-23: imports make_classification_result, make_document, make_settings_obj, make_mock_paperless directly from helpers.
findings.suggestion: Use direct imports in the OCR test file as the classifier tests do, eliminating the redundant wrappers.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient.get_documents_to_process() is an instance method that hardcodes PRE_TAG_ID, but both daemons use iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag which calls get_documents_by_tag directly. This method appears to be dead code that follows a different convention than the rest of the iteration pipeline.
evidence: paperless.py line 103-104: get_documents_to_process() delegates to get_documents_by_tag(self.settings.PRE_TAG_ID). Neither daemon.py (ocr or classifier) calls this method; both use iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag which calls get_documents_by_tag with an explicit tag_id parameter.
findings.suggestion: Remove get_documents_to_process() to reduce confusion about which iteration path is canonical.

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache._get_or_create_item_id uses string attribute names (mapping_attr, items_attr) with getattr() to access instance dictionaries. This indirection exists to share logic across correspondents, document types, and tags, but it trades type safety for DRY. The three public callers pass hardcoded string attribute names, making the abstraction fragile to refactoring.
evidence: taxonomy.py lines 168-209: _get_or_create_item_id takes mapping_attr: str and items_attr: str, uses getattr(self, mapping_attr) on lines 188 and 201, getattr(self, items_attr) on line 206. The three callers (lines 211-233) pass string literals '_correspondent_map', '_correspondents', etc. The get_or_create_tag_ids method (line 235) duplicates much of this logic inline rather than using _get_or_create_item_id, suggesting the abstraction doesn't fully fit.
findings.suggestion: Replace the stringly-typed getattr pattern with a small data structure (e.g. a NamedTuple or dataclass per taxonomy kind) that holds the map, list, normalizer, and creator together. This eliminates the string coupling and makes the code self-documenting.

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin is a mixin class with exactly one method (_create_completion) that depends on a module-level global (_openai_client) and an implicit self.settings protocol. With only two consumers (OcrProvider, ClassificationProvider), the mixin adds indirection without earning its keep compared to a plain function or a composed dependency.
evidence: llm.py lines 30-44: OpenAIChatMixin defines a single method _create_completion that reads a module-level _openai_client. The retry decorator on it requires self.settings to satisfy RetrySettings protocol. OcrProvider and ClassificationProvider both inherit from it solely to get this one method. The mixin pattern is unusual for Python when the shared behavior is a single function.
findings.suggestion: Consider replacing the mixin with a composed LLM client object that is injected into OcrProvider and ClassificationProvider, or a standalone function that accepts explicit parameters. This would eliminate the hidden self.settings coupling and the module-level global.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat catches openai.APIError and returns None instead of raising, while OcrProvider.transcribe_image lets openai.APIError propagate (after retries exhaust). This means the two providers handle the same exception class differently at their boundary.
evidence: classifier/provider.py lines 92-95: catches openai.APIError, logs, returns None. ocr/provider.py lines 110-117: catches openai.APIError after the retry decorator, logs, increments stats, but continues the model loop; if all models fail it returns REFUSAL_MARK. Both eventually produce a non-exception sentinel on total failure, but the classifier uses None while OCR uses (REFUSAL_MARK, ''). The inconsistency is in the error strategy: classifier returns None for any single model failure allowing fallback, while OCR catches APIError and does the same, but their sentinel values and paths diverge.
findings.suggestion: Unify the model-fallback loop pattern. Both providers iterate models and fall back. Extract a shared 'try each model' helper that takes a per-model callable, standardizing how APIError, BadRequestError, and parse errors are handled across both providers.

findings.summary: Two module-level singletons (_openai_holder in llm.py, llm_limiter in concurrency.py) require explicit .init() calls at startup before they are usable. This creates an implicit initialization ordering contract that is enforced only by bootstrap_daemon's call sequence, not by the type system or any structural guarantee.
evidence: llm.py line 42 instantiates _openai_holder = _OpenAIClientHolder() at module level with _client=None. Its .get() raises RuntimeError if called before .init(). concurrency.py line 40 instantiates llm_limiter = LLMConcurrencyLimiter() with _semaphore=None. Both are initialized during bootstrap via setup_libraries() and llm_limiter.init() respectively. Any code importing llm_limiter or _openai_holder that runs before bootstrap will silently get a no-op limiter or a RuntimeError.
findings.suggestion: Consider using dependency injection to pass configured instances into the providers rather than relying on module-level singletons with two-phase initialization. The providers already accept settings via constructor; they could accept the client and limiter similarly.

findings.summary: The @retry decorator has an implicit protocol dependency: it requires that the decorated method's owner has a .settings attribute satisfying RetrySettings. This couples every retried class (PaperlessClient, OcrProvider, ClassificationProvider via mixin) to a specific attribute naming convention that is not enforced at class definition time.
evidence: retry.py line 33: 'settings: RetrySettings = self.settings'. The RetrySettings Protocol (line 16-18) is defined but never used as a type annotation on any class. If a class using @retry names its settings differently or removes it, the failure is a runtime AttributeError with no static analysis warning.
findings.suggestion: Pass retry parameters explicitly to the decorator (e.g., @retry(max_retries=..., max_backoff=...)) or accept a settings argument in the decorator factory, removing the implicit self.settings contract.

findings.summary: The unique_models() function in llm.py is a 7-line reimplementation of order-preserving deduplication that Python's dict.fromkeys() handles in one expression.
evidence: llm.py lines 66-74: unique_models() manually tracks a 'seen' set and 'unique' list. The equivalent is list(dict.fromkeys(models)), which preserves insertion order in Python 3.7+. The project requires Python 3.11+.
findings.suggestion: Replace the body with 'return list(dict.fromkeys(models))'.

findings.summary: At least 8 test files define their own local _make_settings() wrapper that simply delegates to the centralized make_settings_obj() factory. This is boilerplate duplication that obscures which test files use shared infrastructure and which have genuinely custom setup.
evidence: test_worker.py line 37: 'def _make_settings(**overrides): from tests.helpers.factories import make_settings_obj; return make_settings_obj(**overrides)'. Identical pattern in test_provider.py:34, test_daemon.py:29, test_paperless.py:26, test_ocr_workflow.py:32, test_classifier_workflow.py:25. Similarly, _make_mock_paperless wrappers in test_worker.py:42 and test_daemon.py:34 just forward to make_mock_paperless. These wrappers add zero logic.
findings.suggestion: Import make_settings_obj and make_mock_paperless directly where needed, or define them as shared pytest fixtures in conftest.py. The pass-through wrappers add indirection without value.

findings.summary: Tests for worker classes extensively patch internal private methods by name (e.g., _ocr_pages_in_parallel, _update_paperless_document, _finalize_with_error), making them fragile to any refactoring of the worker's internal decomposition.
evidence: test_worker.py patches private methods like '_ocr_pages_in_parallel', '_update_paperless_document', and '_finalize_with_error' as test boundaries. These are implementation details of DocumentProcessor. Renaming or inlining any of these private methods breaks multiple tests even if external behavior is unchanged.
findings.suggestion: Where possible, test through the public process() method with controlled mocks at the real external boundaries (PaperlessClient, OcrProvider) rather than patching internal private methods. Reserve private method testing for complex internal logic that warrants isolation.

findings.summary: The thread-pool concurrency code in daemon_loop.py has no tests for race conditions, shutdown during active processing, or thread pool exhaustion. Tests mock the executor or test single-threaded behavior only.
evidence: daemon_loop.py uses ThreadPoolExecutor with as_completed for concurrent document processing. test_daemon_loop.py tests _process_batch, _poll_once, and run_polling_threadpool but uses synchronous process_item callables that complete immediately. No test verifies behavior when items are processed concurrently with competing threads, or when shutdown is requested while items are in-flight in the pool.
findings.suggestion: Add integration-level tests that use real ThreadPoolExecutor with concurrent work items and verify correct behavior under shutdown signals and item failures happening simultaneously.

findings.summary: test_config.py hardcodes expected default values (tag IDs, timeouts, model lists) that mirror the source. When a default changes in config.py, the test must be updated in lockstep, but the test does not verify any behavior -- just that a constant equals another constant.
evidence: test_config.py line 47: 'assert s.PAPERLESS_URL == "http://paperless:8000"'. Line 55: 'assert s.AI_MODELS == ["gpt-5-mini", "gpt-5.2", "o4-mini"]'. These directly restate the default values from config.py lines 10-11 and 100. Changes to defaults require updating both files, and the tests do not verify any interesting behavior (validation, parsing, override logic).
findings.suggestion: Focus Settings default tests on behavioral properties (e.g., 'default AI_MODELS has at least one entry', 'default POLL_INTERVAL is positive') rather than pinning exact default values. Test override and validation logic instead.

findings.summary: Test files begin with large docstrings that enumerate every test case in the file. These docstrings restate the test class and method names, adding maintenance burden without providing information that the test names don't already convey.
evidence: test_worker.py lines 1-20: A 20-line docstring listing every test scenario ('process() happy path: full pipeline success', 'process() claim fails -> returns early', etc.). test_provider.py lines 1-16: Similar enumeration. These are maintenance liabilities -- adding or removing a test requires updating the docstring, and they are never rendered in test output. The test method names already describe each scenario.
findings.suggestion: Replace exhaustive test-listing docstrings with a brief one-liner stating what module is under test. Let the test names speak for themselves.

findings.summary: Tests are organized into single-method classes (one class per scenario) throughout the test suite. This pattern inflates file sizes, adds indentation overhead, and produces no benefit over flat functions when the classes share no state or fixtures.
evidence: test_config.py uses classes like TestDefaults, TestPaperlessUrl, TestLlmProvider, etc., each containing a handful of flat test methods that share no setup, fixtures, or state. Most classes have no __init__, no class fixtures, no setUp -- they just namespace tests that could be top-level functions. This is consistent across nearly every test file in the project.
findings.suggestion: Use classes only when tests share fixtures or setup (via class-scoped fixtures or setUp). For isolated test functions, flat module-level test functions are simpler and more Pythonic.

findings.summary: Source and test files use heavy ASCII banner comments (lines of dashes, equals signs) to separate logical sections. These visual separators add noise and are hallmarks of AI-generated code structure.
findings.suggestion: Remove banner comments. Python code should be organized by blank lines and clear naming. If a file needs heavy visual separation, it may need to be split into smaller modules instead.

findings.summary: Several trivial functions have multi-line docstrings that restate what the code does without adding any insight about why or what constraints apply.
evidence: llm.py line 46: 'def init_openai_client(client: openai.OpenAI) -> None:' with docstring 'Store the configured OpenAI client for use by the mixin.' -- the function name already says this. llm.py line 67: 'def unique_models(models: list[str]) -> list[str]:' with docstring 'Deduplicate a model list while preserving insertion order.' -- also self-evident. test_provider.py line 49: '_make_response' with docstring 'Create a mock OpenAI chat completion response.' -- restates the function name. test_provider.py line 57: '_make_test_image' with docstring 'Create a non-blank test image.' -- obvious from the function name and body.
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings from functions whose names and signatures already communicate their purpose. Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behavior, constraints, or side effects.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient has debug-level log lines on nearly every method entry point, logging the URL for routine operations. This entry/exit logging pattern on straightforward HTTP wrapper methods is characteristic of AI-generated code.
evidence: paperless.py line 124: log.debug('Fetching documents with tag', ..., url=url). Line 129: log.debug('Fetching document', doc_id=doc_id, url=url). Line 196: log.debug('Listing correspondents', url=url). Line 201: log.debug('Listing document types', url=url). Line 206: log.debug('Listing tags', url=url). Every HTTP method logs its URL at debug level, even though the retry decorator already logs on failure and the URL is constructable from doc_id.
findings.suggestion: Remove entry-point debug logs from simple HTTP wrappers. The retry decorator and error paths already provide sufficient observability. Keep info-level logs only for significant state changes (update, create).
